content:
# Acts Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios from Quire 86 (PDF pages 228-229) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_004_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_004_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

### Peter and John Arrested

1 Now while they were speaking to the people, the priests and the captain of the temple and the Sadducees came upon them,

2 being greatly disturbed because they were teaching the people and proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection from the dead.

3 And they laid hands on them and placed them in custody until the next day, for it was already evening.

4 But many of those who had heard the word believed, and the number of the men came to be about five thousand.

### Before the Sanhedrin

5 And it came to pass on the next day that their rulers and the elders and the scribes were gathered together in Jerusalem,

6 and Annas the high priest, and Caiaphas, and John, and Alexander, and as many as were of the high-priestly family.

7 And having set them in the midst, they inquired, "By what power or by what name did you do this?"

8 Then Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, said to them, "Rulers of the people and elders,

9 if we are being examined today concerning a good deed done to a sick man, by what means this man has been healed,

10 let it be known to all of you and to all the people of Israel, that by the name of Jesus Christ the Nazarene, whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead — by him this man stands before you in good health.

11 This is the stone that was rejected by you, the builders, which has become the cornerstone.

12 And there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved."

### The Council's Response

13 Now when they observed the boldness of Peter and John, and perceived that they were unlearned and ordinary men, they marveled, and they recognized that they had been with Jesus.

14 And seeing the man who had been healed standing with them, they had nothing to say in opposition.

15 But having commanded them to go outside the council, they conferred with one another,

16 saying, "What shall we do with these men? For that a notable sign has been done through them is evident to all who dwell in Jerusalem, and we cannot deny it.

17 But in order that it may spread no further among the people, let us warn them with threats to speak no more to anyone in this name."

18 And having called them, they commanded them not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus.

19 But Peter and John answered and said to them, "Whether it is right in the sight of God to listen to you rather than to God, you must judge,

20 for we cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard."

21 And when they had further threatened them, they released them, finding no way to punish them, on account of the people, for all were glorifying God for what had happened.

22 For the man on whom this sign of healing had been performed was more than forty years old.

### The Believers' Prayer

23 And when they were released, they went to their own people and reported all that the chief priests and the elders had said to them.

24 And when they heard it, they lifted their voice together to God and said, "Sovereign Lord, you who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and all that is in them,

25 who through the Holy Spirit, by the mouth of our father David your servant, said,

'Why did the nations rage, and the peoples devise futile things?

26 The kings of the earth set themselves in array, and the rulers were gathered together, against the Lord and against his Christ' —

27 for truly in this city there were gathered together against your holy servant Jesus, whom you anointed, both Herod and Pontius Pilate, with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel,

28 to do whatever your hand and your purpose had predetermined to take place.

29 And now, Lord, look upon their threats, and grant to your servants to speak your word with all boldness,

30 while you stretch out your hand for healing, and signs and wonders are performed through the name of your holy servant Jesus."

31 And when they had prayed, the place in which they were gathered together was shaken, and they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and spoke the word of God with boldness.

### All Things in Common

32 Now the multitude of those who believed were of one heart and soul, and no one said that any of the things he possessed was his own, but they had all things in common.

33 And with great power the apostles were giving their testimony to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus, and great grace was upon them all.

34 For there was not a needy person among them, for as many as were owners of lands or houses sold them and brought the proceeds of what was sold

35 and laid them at the feet of the apostles, and it was distributed to each as anyone had need.

36 And Joseph, who was also called Barnabas by the apostles (which is translated Son of Encouragement), a Levite, a Cypriot by birth,

37 having sold a field that belonged to him, brought the money and laid it at the feet of the apostles.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**General agreement:** The three translators show strong alignment across Acts 4. The arrest narrative (vv. 1-4), the Sanhedrin hearing (vv. 5-22), the believers' prayer (vv. 23-31), and the community of goods (vv. 32-37) are rendered consistently in substance across all three agents. Most differences are matters of register and style rather than meaning. EMET preserves Greek morphological structure and word order; LOGOS provides formal English equivalence with clarity; PNEUMA aims for contemporary accessibility and dramatic impact. The Sinaiticus manuscript images confirm the standard text throughout this chapter.

**Manuscript observations:** The first image (PDF page 228) shows the rightmost column beginning approximately at Acts 4:4, with the transition from Acts 3 visible in the preceding columns. The second image (PDF page 229) covers Acts 4:11 through the end of the chapter and into Acts 5, spread across four columns. The uncial text is legible and consistent with the standard Sinaiticus transcription. No significant corrections or scribal alterations are visible in the Acts 4 portion. The text flow confirms the standard reading at all points of translator divergence examined below.

**Key decisions:**

1. **Verse 2 — "proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection from the dead" vs. "proclaiming that in Jesus the dead are raised to life":** EMET: "the proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection, the [one] out-of [the] dead." LOGOS: "proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection from the dead." PNEUMA: "proclaiming that in Jesus the dead are raised to life." The Greek reads καταγγέλλειν ἐν τῷ Ἰησοῦ τὴν ἀνάστασιν τὴν ἐκ νεκρῶν. The phrase ἐν τῷ Ἰησοῦ ("in Jesus") modifies the proclamation — they were announcing, using Jesus as the evidence or basis, the resurrection from the dead. LOGOS captures this construction most faithfully. PNEUMA's expansion ("that in Jesus the dead are raised to life") is interpretively accurate but restructures the Greek phrasing. I follow LOGOS.

2. **Verse 9 — "healed" vs. "saved" (σέσωται):** EMET: "has-been-saved." LOGOS: "has been healed." PNEUMA: "how he was healed." The Greek σῴζω means both "to save" and "to heal/make well." In context, Peter is speaking about the physical healing of the lame man, so "healed" is the immediate referent. However, Peter will pivot to salvation in verse 12, creating a deliberate wordplay in the Greek that cannot be fully preserved in English. EMET's "saved" preserves the theological double meaning, but I follow LOGOS with "healed" for the immediate context, noting the wordplay in this council note. The Sinaiticus reading is σεσωται, confirming the standard text.

3. **Verse 10 — "in good health" vs. "healthy" vs. "completely well" (ὑγιής):** EMET: "healthy." LOGOS: "in good health." PNEUMA: "completely well." The Greek ὑγιής means "sound, healthy, whole." LOGOS's "in good health" provides appropriate formal register for a judicial address. PNEUMA's "completely well" adds an intensifier not in the Greek. I follow LOGOS.

4. **Verse 11 — "cornerstone" vs. "head of the corner" (κεφαλὴν γωνίας):** EMET: "head of-[the] corner." LOGOS: "cornerstone." PNEUMA: "cornerstone." The Greek κεφαλὴν γωνίας literally means "head of the corner" — the chief stone at the corner or apex of a building. "Cornerstone" is the standard English translation that conveys this architectural metaphor. EMET's literal rendering preserves the Greek structure but is obscure in English. I follow the LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus with "cornerstone."

5. **Verse 11 — "rejected" vs. "treated with contempt" (ἐξουθενηθείς):** EMET: "having-been-treated-with-contempt." LOGOS: "rejected." PNEUMA: "rejected." The Greek ἐξουθενέω means "to treat as nothing, to despise, to reject with contempt." It is a stronger word than simple rejection — it carries overtones of scorn. However, the Psalm 118:22 quotation is traditionally rendered with "rejected" in English, and both LOGOS and PNEUMA use this. I follow the consensus with "rejected," which captures the essential meaning while maintaining the recognizable scriptural echo.

6. **Verse 13 — "unlearned and ordinary" vs. "unlettered and untrained" (ἀγράμματοί... καὶ ἰδιῶται):** EMET: "unlettered men and untrained." LOGOS: "unlearned and ordinary men." PNEUMA: "uneducated, ordinary men." The Greek ἀγράμματος means "unlettered, illiterate" (without γράμματα, formal learning), and ἰδιώτης means "a private person, layman, one without professional knowledge." LOGOS's "unlearned and ordinary" captures both terms clearly. EMET's "unlettered and untrained" is precise but archaic. PNEUMA's "uneducated, ordinary" is close to LOGOS. I follow LOGOS with the minor adjustment of "observed" for "saw" (from EMET's κατα-λαμβάνω, which implies careful observation).

7. **Verse 17 — "warn them with threats" vs. "threaten them with a threat" (ἀπειλησώμεθα... ἀπειλῇ):** EMET: "let-us-threaten them with-a-threat." LOGOS: "let us warn them with threats." PNEUMA: "let us warn them with threats." The Greek uses the cognate dative construction ἀπειλῇ ἀπειλησώμεθα (a Semitic intensifying construction: "let us threaten with a threat"). LOGOS and PNEUMA both render this as "warn them with threats," which conveys the seriousness without the awkwardness of the literal cognate construction. I follow the LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus.

8. **Verse 19 — "right" vs. "righteous" (δίκαιον):** EMET: "righteous." LOGOS: "right." PNEUMA: "right." The Greek δίκαιος means "righteous, just, right." In this context — a rhetorical question about proper conduct before God — "right" is the natural English choice. "Righteous" (EMET) carries a more theological register that, while lexically valid, overweights the adjective in this conversational context. I follow LOGOS and PNEUMA with "right."

9. **Verse 24 — "Sovereign Lord" vs. "Master" (Δέσποτα):** EMET: "Master." LOGOS: "Sovereign Lord." PNEUMA: "Sovereign Lord." The Greek δεσπότης means "master, lord, sovereign" — one who has absolute authority. In prayer address, "Sovereign Lord" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) better conveys the sense of divine sovereignty being invoked, especially as the prayer proceeds to discuss God's control over history. "Master" (EMET) is lexically accurate but in modern English lacks the gravitas of the Greek. I follow the LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus.

10. **Verse 25 — Textual difficulty:** This verse is notoriously difficult in Greek, with a complex relative clause construction. EMET renders it woodenly: "the [one] through [the] Holy Spirit [by the] mouth of-David our father, your servant, having-said." LOGOS: "who through the Holy Spirit, by the mouth of our father David your servant, said." PNEUMA: "You spoke through the Holy Spirit by the mouth of our father David, your servant." The Sinaiticus text at this point confirms the standard (difficult) reading. I follow LOGOS, which navigates the syntactical complexity while preserving the relative clause structure that connects God's sovereignty (v. 24) to David's prophetic speech.

11. **Verse 25 — "futile things" vs. "empty things" vs. "in vain" (κενά):** EMET: "empty-[things]." LOGOS: "futile things." PNEUMA: "in vain." The Greek κενά means "empty, vain, futile" — describing the plans of the peoples as amounting to nothing. "Futile things" (LOGOS) renders the adjective substantively and captures the sense of purposelessness. PNEUMA's adverbial "in vain" shifts the syntax. EMET's "empty things" is literal. I follow LOGOS with "futile things" for the Psalm 2:1 quotation.

12. **Verse 26 — "set themselves in array" vs. "stood near" vs. "took their stand" (παρέστησαν):** EMET: "stood-near." LOGOS: "set themselves in array." PNEUMA: "took their stand." The Greek παρίστημι means "to stand beside, to present oneself, to stand ready." In the LXX of Psalm 2:2, it renders the Hebrew יִתְיַצְּבוּ ("stationed themselves"). LOGOS's "set themselves in array" adds a military nuance not strictly in the Greek. PNEUMA's "took their stand" is strong and clear. I follow PNEUMA here: "took their stand" conveys deliberate, defiant positioning without over-translating.

13. **Verse 27 — "Gentiles" vs. "nations" (ἐθνῶν):** EMET: "nations." LOGOS: "Gentiles." PNEUMA: "Gentile nations." The Greek ἔθνος means "nation, people" and in Jewish usage often specifically "Gentile nations." In verse 25-26, the same word (ἔθνη) is translated "nations" in the Psalm quotation. Verse 27 applies the psalm to the historical situation. LOGOS's "Gentiles" identifies the referent (non-Jewish peoples represented by Pilate), while EMET's "nations" maintains lexical consistency with the quotation. I follow LOGOS with "Gentiles" for verse 27, since the application makes the referent specific.

14. **Verse 28 — "predetermined" vs. "predestined" (προώρισεν):** EMET: "predetermined." LOGOS: "predestined." PNEUMA: "determined beforehand." The Greek προορίζω means "to decide upon beforehand, to predetermine." Both "predestined" and "predetermined" are valid. "Predetermined" (EMET) is slightly more neutral than "predestined" (LOGOS), which carries strong theological freight from later doctrinal debates. PNEUMA's "determined beforehand" is a paraphrase of the same. I adopt "predetermined" with EMET, as it accurately renders the Greek prefix πρό- ("before") + ὁρίζω ("to set bounds, determine") without importing the loaded theological term "predestined."

15. **Verse 31 — "spoke" vs. "continued to speak" vs. "began to speak" (ἐλάλουν):** EMET: "they-were-speaking" (imperfect). LOGOS: "continued to speak" (imperfect, continuative). PNEUMA: "began to speak" (ingressive). The Greek ἐλάλουν is imperfect tense, which can be continuative ("kept speaking") or ingressive ("began to speak"). In context, the Spirit-filling inaugurates a new phase of bold proclamation, so the ingressive sense is attractive (PNEUMA). However, the imperfect more likely indicates ongoing action — they were speaking God's word with boldness as their characteristic activity. I render simply "spoke" to preserve the ambiguity of the Greek imperfect, allowing the reader to perceive both nuances.

16. **Verse 32 — "one heart and soul" vs. "one in heart and mind":** EMET: "the heart and the soul was one." LOGOS: "of one heart and soul." PNEUMA: "one in heart and mind." The Greek ἦν καρδία καὶ ψυχὴ μία — literally "was heart and soul one." LOGOS's "of one heart and soul" is the most natural English rendering that preserves both Greek terms (καρδία and ψυχή). PNEUMA substitutes "mind" for "soul" (ψυχή), which narrows the meaning. I follow LOGOS.

17. **Verse 34 — "for" vs. "because" at the start of the causal clause:** EMET: "for as-many-as." LOGOS: "for as many as." PNEUMA: "because everyone who." All three correctly identify the causal relationship. LOGOS's "for as many as" best preserves the Greek ὅσοι γάρ construction. PNEUMA's "because everyone who" paraphrases. I follow LOGOS.

18. **Verse 36 — "Cypriot" vs. "Cyprian" (Κύπριος):** EMET: "Cyprian." LOGOS: "Cypriot." PNEUMA: "from Cyprus." The standard English demonym for someone from Cyprus is "Cypriot." EMET's "Cyprian" is an older form that may also be confused with the Church Father Cyprian. I follow LOGOS with "Cypriot."

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 2 — Structure of the proclamation clause

**The variant:** How to render the phrase ἐν τῷ Ἰησοῦ in relation to the resurrection proclamation.

- **EMET** rendered "the proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection, the [one] out-of [the] dead" (preserving Greek word order).
- **LOGOS** rendered "proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection from the dead" (formal equivalence).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "proclaiming that in Jesus the dead are raised to life" (restructured for clarity).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS. The Greek construction καταγγέλλειν ἐν τῷ Ἰησοῦ τὴν ἀνάστασιν τὴν ἐκ νεκρῶν is preserved with its prepositional structure intact. PNEUMA's restructuring, while interpretively sound, transforms a proclamation about the resurrection into a statement about the dead being raised, subtly shifting the emphasis.

### Verse 9 — σέσωται: "healed" or "saved"?

**The variant:** The Greek σῴζω carries both physical ("heal") and spiritual ("save") meanings.

- **EMET** rendered "has-been-saved" (theological sense).
- **LOGOS** rendered "has been healed" (physical sense).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "how he was healed" (physical sense).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA with "healed." In context, Peter is responding to an inquiry about the physical healing of the lame man. However, the Greek wordplay is deliberate: Peter uses σῴζω here and again in verse 12 (δεῖ σωθῆναι ἡμᾶς, "we must be saved"), pivoting from physical healing to spiritual salvation. EMET's "saved" preserves this connection but obscures the immediate physical referent. English cannot capture both senses in a single word, so "healed" is chosen for verse 9 and "saved" for verse 12, with the wordplay noted.

### Verse 11 — ἐξουθενηθείς: "rejected" or "treated with contempt"?

**The variant:** The participle carries different degrees of severity.

- **EMET** rendered "having-been-treated-with-contempt" (full semantic range).
- **LOGOS** rendered "rejected" (standard Psalm 118 translation).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "rejected" (standard).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA with "rejected." The Greek ἐξουθενέω is indeed stronger than simple rejection — it means "to treat as nothing, to despise utterly." EMET's rendering captures more of this contempt. However, the verse quotes Psalm 118:22 (LXX 117:22), and "rejected" is the established English rendering of this psalm. Maintaining the recognizable scriptural echo takes priority here.

### Verse 11 — κεφαλὴν γωνίας: "cornerstone" or "head of the corner"?

**The variant:** The architectural metaphor is rendered literally or idiomatically.

- **EMET** rendered "head of-[the] corner" (literal).
- **LOGOS** rendered "cornerstone" (idiomatic).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "cornerstone" (idiomatic).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA with "cornerstone." The Greek κεφαλὴν γωνίας literally means "head of the corner" — the chief stone at the corner or apex. "Cornerstone" is the standard English translation that conveys the essential meaning. EMET's literal rendering preserves the Greek structure but is architecturally obscure to modern readers.

### Verse 13 — ἀγράμματοι καὶ ἰδιῶται: rendering of the paired description

**The variant:** The description of Peter and John's lack of formal training is rendered differently.

- **EMET** rendered "unlettered men and untrained."
- **LOGOS** rendered "unlearned and ordinary men."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "uneducated, ordinary men."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "unlearned and ordinary men." The Greek ἀγράμματος ("without letters," i.e., without formal scribal education) and ἰδιώτης ("layman, private person, non-professional") describe men without rabbinic training. "Unlearned" captures ἀγράμματος without implying total illiteracy, and "ordinary" captures ἰδιώτης as non-specialists. EMET's "unlettered" is precise but archaic; "untrained" for ἰδιώτης misses the sense of social ordinariness.

### Verse 19 — δίκαιον: "right" or "righteous"?

**The variant:** The adjective carries both ethical and theological weight.

- **EMET** rendered "righteous before God."
- **LOGOS** rendered "right in the sight of God."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "right before God."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "right in the sight of God." The Greek δίκαιον ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ is a question of ethical propriety, not a statement about the theological status of righteousness. "Right" suits the rhetorical context of Peter's challenge to the council. "Righteous" (EMET) over-theologizes the adjective in this conversational setting.

### Verse 24 — Δέσποτα: "Sovereign Lord" or "Master"?

**The variant:** The prayer address uses δεσπότης, a term of absolute authority.

- **EMET** rendered "Master."
- **LOGOS** rendered "Sovereign Lord."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "Sovereign Lord."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA with "Sovereign Lord." The Greek δεσπότης denotes one who has complete ownership and authority. In the context of a prayer that proceeds to declare God's creative sovereignty (v. 24b) and providential control over history (vv. 27-28), "Sovereign Lord" captures the theological weight of the address. "Master" (EMET) is lexically valid but in modern English carries connotations of servitude rather than cosmic sovereignty.

### Verse 26 — παρέστησαν: "set themselves in array" or "took their stand"?

**The variant:** The verb describing the kings' opposition is rendered with varying military overtones.

- **EMET** rendered "stood-near."
- **LOGOS** rendered "set themselves in array."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "took their stand."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows PNEUMA with "took their stand." The Greek παρίστημι means "to stand beside, to present oneself." In the LXX of Psalm 2:2, it renders Hebrew יִתְיַצְּבוּ ("stationed themselves"). EMET's "stood near" is too mild — it misses the hostile posturing. LOGOS's "set themselves in array" imports military formation language beyond the Greek. PNEUMA's "took their stand" conveys deliberate, defiant opposition without over-translating.

### Verse 28 — προώρισεν: "predetermined" or "predestined"?

**The variant:** The theological term for God's prior decision is rendered with different English equivalents.

- **EMET** rendered "predetermined."
- **LOGOS** rendered "predestined."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "determined beforehand."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET with "predetermined." The Greek προορίζω (πρό- + ὁρίζω) means "to determine beforehand, to set bounds in advance." Both "predestined" and "predetermined" are valid. However, "predestined" carries heavy theological freight from later Augustinian and Reformed debates about election and free will. In the Acts 4 prayer, the believers are affirming God's sovereign control over the events of Jesus's death — not articulating a doctrine of individual predestination. "Predetermined" is more neutral and allows the text to speak on its own terms.

### Verse 31 — ἐλάλουν: imperfect aspect — "spoke," "continued to speak," or "began to speak"?

**The variant:** The imperfect tense is interpreted with different aspectual nuances.

- **EMET** rendered "they-were-speaking" (imperfect, descriptive).
- **LOGOS** rendered "continued to speak" (continuative imperfect).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "began to speak" (ingressive imperfect).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus renders "spoke" — a simple English past tense that preserves the ambiguity of the Greek imperfect. LOGOS's "continued" implies resumption of a prior activity; PNEUMA's "began" implies initiation of a new activity. Both are valid interpretive options for the imperfect ἐλάλουν, but since the context could support either reading (the believers had been bold before, but the Spirit-filling renews and intensifies their proclamation), the unadorned "spoke" allows the reader to perceive both nuances without the translator making the decision for them.

### Verse 32 — ψυχή: "soul" or "mind"?

**The variant:** The Greek ψυχή in the phrase "one heart and soul" is rendered differently.

- **EMET** rendered "the heart and the soul was one."
- **LOGOS** rendered "of one heart and soul."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "one in heart and mind."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "of one heart and soul." The Greek ψυχή means "soul, life, self" — the animating principle of a person. PNEUMA's substitution of "mind" narrows ψυχή to the cognitive faculty, losing the broader sense of the whole inner person. The traditional pairing "heart and soul" is both faithful to the Greek and deeply rooted in English biblical idiom.

---

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
